stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after eating a peanut butter sandwich at a birthday party. His parents say his lips swelled, he developed a widespread itchy rash, and he started coughing and saying his throat felt funny on the drive in. He has a known peanut allergy and carries an EpiPen Jr at preschool, but it was not used at the party.

On arrival he is tachypnoeic with audible wheeze and is anxious but answering questions. There is no stridor and no drooling. His weight on the resus chart is 18 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 148 /min, blood pressure 86/54 mmHg, respiratory rate 36 /min, SpO2 94% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: diffuse urticaria over trunk and face, periorbital and lip swelling, bilateral expiratory wheeze, chest moving symmetrically, no stridor
data_table.Investigations: clinical diagnosis, no bloods drawn yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate adrenaline dose and route?

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 150 micrograms (0.15 mL of 1 in 1000) into the anterolateral thigh
options.rationale: An 18 kg child falls within the ASCIA 10 to 20 kg band, which prescribes 150 micrograms IM (EpiPen Jr equivalent) into the mid anterolateral thigh. The banded table is the AU standard and avoids the dosing errors that occur with weight-based calculation in a stressed resus. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 300 micrograms (0.3 mL of 1 in 1000) into the anterolateral thigh
options.rationale: 300 micrograms is the ASCIA dose for the 20 kg and above band. At 18 kg this is twice the indicated dose and risks tachyarrhythmia and hypertension. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Intravenous adrenaline 18 micrograms (1.8 mL of 1 in 100 000) as a slow bolus
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis after two IM doses have failed and must be given as a diluted infusion with cardiac monitoring. First-line is always IM into the anterolateral thigh. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 180 micrograms (0.18 mL of 1 in 1000) at 10 micrograms per kg
options.rationale: Subcutaneous absorption is slower and less reliable than IM in shock. ASCIA explicitly recommends IM (not subcut) in the anterolateral thigh. Weight-based 10 microgram per kg arithmetic is also not the ASCIA standard. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 via face mask with high-flow oxygen
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline treats upper airway oedema in croup, not systemic anaphylaxis. It does not address hypotension, shock, or peripheral vasodilation and delays definitive IM dosing. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines; RCH CPG: Anaphylaxis)
source_refs: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

explanation.summary: ASCIA anaphylaxis dosing uses a banded age and weight table rather than per kg arithmetic to reduce dosing errors during resuscitation. The 10 to 20 kg band receives 150 micrograms IM (the EpiPen Jr dose); the 20 kg and above band receives 300 micrograms IM (the standard EpiPen dose). All children with airway, breathing, or circulatory features get IM adrenaline into the anterolateral thigh as the first action, before fluids or steroids. Repeat at 5 minutes if no improvement; consider IV infusion after two failed IM doses.
key_points: ASCIA uses a banded IM dose table: 10 to 20 kg gets 150 micrograms, 20 kg and above gets 300 micrograms
Anterolateral thigh IM is faster and more reliable than subcutaneous or deltoid injection
IV adrenaline is third-line and only after two failed IM doses, as a diluted infusion with monitoring
Nebulised adrenaline treats upper airway obstruction, not systemic anaphylaxis
explanation.pearls: ASCIA IM adrenaline by band: 10 to 20 kg gets 150 micrograms (EpiPen Jr); 20 kg and above gets 300 micrograms (EpiPen). Repeat IM at 5 minutes if no improvement before escalating to an IV infusion.

sources.label: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines

stem:
A 3-year-old girl is brought to a regional emergency department with three days of fever and one day of lethargy and reduced oral intake. Her parents say she has been wetting fewer nappies and is harder to rouse this afternoon.

She responds to voice but is mottled to the knees. Capillary refill is 4 seconds centrally. Her weight is 14 kg. Peripheral access is being attempted; the paediatric registrar is at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 168 /min, blood pressure 78/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 40 /min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 39.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: mottled peripheries, prolonged central capillary refill, dry mucous membranes, no rash, soft abdomen, chest clear
data_table.Investigations: venous gas lactate 4.2 mmol/L; FBC, UEC, blood cultures pending

lead_in: After IV access is obtained, what is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Take blood cultures then give IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg, then a 20 mL/kg saline bolus
options.rationale: The RCH paediatric sepsis pathway sequences IV/IO access first, then blood cultures, then the first antibiotic dose within the hour, then a 20 mL/kg saline bolus over 5 to 10 minutes (repeat to 60 mL/kg). Cultures are taken before antibiotics only if it does not delay them. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management

options.text: Give a 20 mL/kg saline bolus, then blood cultures, then IV ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg
options.rationale: Although fluids and antibiotics both belong in the first hour, RCH places the first antibiotic dose immediately after cultures and before the fluid bolus where access permits. Delaying antibiotics for a bolus worsens mortality in septic shock. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management

options.text: Start a peripheral adrenaline infusion at 0.05 micrograms per kg per minute now
options.rationale: Inotropes are reserved for fluid-refractory shock (no improvement after 40 to 60 mL/kg). Starting adrenaline before any fluid is given inverts the RCH and ANZICS paediatric sepsis pathway. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 2 mg/kg as the priority before antibiotics or fluids
options.rationale: Stress-dose steroids are considered only in catecholamine-refractory shock or known adrenal insufficiency. Empiric steroids are not first-line and delay the source-directed antibiotic. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management

options.text: Give a 10 mL/kg saline bolus over 10 minutes and reassess before any antibiotic
options.rationale: A 10 mL/kg bolus is the RCH starting volume for DKA and for neonates with suspected cardiac disease, not for paediatric septic shock. Antibiotics must not wait for the bolus to complete. (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management)
source_refs: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management

explanation.summary: The RCH paediatric sepsis pathway runs as a sequenced first-hour bundle: recognise sepsis, secure IV/IO access, draw blood cultures, give the first IV antibiotic within the hour, and start 20 mL/kg saline boluses over 5 to 10 minutes up to 60 mL/kg. Inotropes follow fluid-refractory shock. The order matters because each step has an independent mortality signal and antibiotics should not be held for cultures if access is difficult.
key_points: Order: IV access, cultures, antibiotics, then 20 mL/kg saline bolus over 5 to 10 minutes
Up to 60 mL/kg of crystalloid before declaring fluid-refractory shock and starting inotropes
10 mL/kg is the DKA and neonatal-cardiac starting bolus, not the septic shock bolus
Antibiotics within the first hour of recognition; do not delay for cultures if access is hard
explanation.pearls: RCH paediatric sepsis: 20 mL/kg saline bolus over 5 to 10 minutes (up to 60 mL/kg total), first IV antibiotic within the hour, inotropes only after fluid-refractory shock. Do not delay antibiotics for cultures if access is difficult.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Sepsis - assessment and management

stem:
A 2-year-old girl has had two days of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and intermittent vomiting after her older brother had a similar illness. She has had small sips of water from a bottle but has refused her usual oral rehydration sachets, vomiting them back within minutes. Her parents brought her to the emergency department this afternoon because her nappies have been dry for 8 hours.

She is alert and miserable but consolable. Mucous membranes are dry. Skin turgor is reduced but capillary refill remains 2 seconds. She is estimated at 7% dehydrated. Weight is 12 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 142 /min, blood pressure 92/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 30 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 37.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, capillary refill 2 seconds, sunken eyes, no peritonism, no rash
data_table.Investigations: stool PCR pending; UEC not yet drawn

lead_in: After a single dose of oral ondansetron 2 mg, she vomits her next ORS attempt. What is the most appropriate next rehydration strategy?

options.text: Continued oral ORS in 5 mL aliquots every 5 minutes for a further 4 hours
options.rationale: Two failed oral trials with ondansetron cover indicate that oral rehydration is not succeeding. Persisting risks ongoing losses outpacing intake; RCH directs a route change at this point. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 100 mL/kg over 4 hours plus maintenance fluids
options.rationale: IV is reserved by RCH for severe dehydration, shock, persistent vomiting despite NGT, or electrolyte derangement that needs correction. Jumping to IV bypasses the safer first-line route. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Nasogastric rehydration with oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg per hour for 4 hours
options.rationale: RCH lists NGT rehydration as first-line for mild to moderate dehydration that fails oral trials. Rapid NGT rehydration (typically 25 mL/kg per hour for 4 hours) avoids IV cannulation, preserves gut integrity, and has lower complication rates than IV in head-to-head studies. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Single 20 mL/kg saline bolus and admission for repeat dose oral ondansetron only
options.rationale: A bolus alone does not address ongoing rehydration needs over 4 hours and ondansetron is a single-dose adjunct in RCH, not a repeated antiemetic. The plan misses the actual rehydration step. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Subcutaneous hyaluronidase-assisted rehydration with normal saline over 6 hours
options.rationale: Hypodermoclysis is occasionally used in palliative and aged care settings; it is not a paediatric gastroenteritis option in the RCH cascade. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

explanation.summary: The RCH gastroenteritis pathway places NGT rehydration above IV for mild to moderate dehydration that fails oral trials. NGT delivers ORS at 25 mL/kg per hour for 4 hours, with electrolyte and weight checks, and is associated with lower rates of phlebitis and shorter inpatient stays than IV. IV is for severe dehydration, shock, or NGT failure. A single dose of oral ondansetron (banded by weight) is the only RCH-supported antiemetic.
key_points: NGT rehydration is RCH first-line for mild to moderate dehydration after failed oral trials
Typical NGT rate: 25 mL/kg per hour for 4 hours of ORS
IV is for severe dehydration, shock, or NGT failure
Ondansetron is single-dose by weight band: 2 mg for under 15 kg, 4 mg for 15 to 30 kg
explanation.pearls: RCH gastroenteritis: NGT rehydration first-line for failed oral, ORS at 25 mL/kg per hour for 4 hours. Single dose ondansetron 2 mg for under 15 kg, 4 mg for 15 to 30 kg. IV only for severe dehydration, shock, or NGT failure.

stem:
A 5-week-old boy is brought to the emergency department in the late evening with a rectal temperature of 38.4 degrees Celsius measured at home and confirmed on arrival. He was born at term by spontaneous vaginal delivery, breastfed, and previously well. He has had reduced feeds today and one episode of vomiting; the bottle volumes have dropped from 90 mL to 50 mL over three feeds.

He is alert in the cot but his cry is high-pitched when handled. The anterior fontanelle is soft and flat. There is no rash. The urine dipstick from a clean catch is negative for leukocytes and nitrites. A chest film is clear. He has received his 6-week immunisations 36 hours ago.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 168 /min, blood pressure 76/44 mmHg, respiratory rate 56 /min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 38.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: high-pitched cry, soft fontanelle, no neck stiffness possible to assess in a 5-week-old, peripheries warm, no purpura
data_table.Investigations: FBC WCC 14.8 x10^9/L, CRP 22 mg/L (less than 5), urine dipstick negative, chest film clear, blood cultures pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Procalcitonin level and discharge if below 0.5 micrograms per litre
options.rationale: Procalcitonin can refine risk in 1 to 3 month infants alongside other parameters but cannot exclude meningitis in an unwell appearing 5-week-old. RCH does not endorse single-marker rule out at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Reassure that post-immunisation fever within 48 hours requires no further workup
options.rationale: Post-immunisation fever can occur but RCH explicitly does NOT use the timing as an exclusion criterion for sepsis workup in an infant under 3 months. Clinical features here (high-pitched cry, reduced feeds, raised CRP) demand the full workup. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Catheter urine specimen for culture and discharge home with safety netting
options.rationale: A catheter urine specimen is part of the full septic screen but is not sufficient alone. RCH requires blood cultures and an LP added on top in this age group. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Lumbar puncture for CSF cell count, protein, glucose, gram stain, culture, and HSV PCR
options.rationale: RCH directs a full septic workup including lumbar puncture for any febrile infant under 4 weeks, and strongly recommends it under 3 months when the infant is unwell, has a high-pitched cry, or has a CRP raised above local thresholds. Recent immunisation does not exclude bacterial sepsis at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

options.text: Cranial ultrasound through the anterior fontanelle to exclude intracranial infection
options.rationale: Cranial ultrasound is used for haemorrhage and hydrocephalus screening, not for diagnosing CNS infection. CSF analysis remains the diagnostic test. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile child)

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 3 months are at substantially higher risk of invasive bacterial infection and meningitis than older children. RCH mandates a full septic workup (blood culture, urine catheter specimen, FBC, CRP, and lumbar puncture) for any febrile infant under 4 weeks and for unwell-appearing infants between 1 and 3 months. Empiric IV cefotaxime (or per local pathway) is started after specimens are taken. Recent immunisation is NOT a reason to defer the workup at this age.
key_points: Under 4 weeks: full septic workup including LP regardless of appearance
1 to 3 months: full workup if unwell appearing or raised inflammatory markers
Post-immunisation fever does not exclude sepsis in this age group
Empiric cefotaxime after specimens; ampicillin added if under 4 weeks for Listeria cover
explanation.pearls: Under 3 months febrile: full septic workup including LP if unwell or raised CRP. Empiric IV cefotaxime; add ampicillin if under 4 weeks. Post-immunisation timing does not exempt from workup.

stem:
A 9-month-old boy is brought in by his mother with episodes where he suddenly starts screaming, draws his legs up toward his tummy, and looks pale; between episodes he is settled and breastfeeds normally. The episodes started 14 hours ago and are now every 20 minutes. He passed a smear of dark mucousy stool with a streak of blood in his nappy two hours ago.

On examination during a quiet phase, his abdomen is soft. There is a vague fullness in the right upper quadrant. He is alert and well perfused with normal capillary refill. There is no peritonism and no abdominal distension.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 142 /min, blood pressure 96/58 mmHg, respiratory rate 32 /min, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 37.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: intermittent inconsolable distress with leg drawing, vague RUQ fullness between episodes, soft abdomen, no peritonism
data_table.Investigations: ultrasound abdomen: target lesion in the right upper quadrant with a 3 cm diameter; no free fluid; no pneumoperitoneum on supine abdominal film

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Trial of conservative management with bowel rest and observation for 24 hours
options.rationale: Untreated intussusception progresses to bowel ischaemia, perforation, and shock. Conservative observation is not a valid strategy once the diagnosis is confirmed on ultrasound. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Immediate laparotomy for manual reduction by the paediatric surgical team
options.rationale: Surgery is the next step only after failed enema reduction, peritonism, perforation, or a pathological lead point on imaging. Going directly to laparotomy in a stable child with a successfully imaged target lesion exposes the child to an unnecessary operation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: IV access and pneumatic reduction under fluoroscopy by paediatric radiology
options.rationale: Stable ileocolic intussusception without peritonism or perforation is treated by air (pneumatic) enema reduction. RCH and the Royal Australasian College of Surgeons paediatric guidance prefer pneumatic over hydrostatic in most centres given higher reduction rates and lower contamination risk. Surgery is reserved for failed enema, peritonism, or perforation. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound in 6 hours to confirm the lesion before any intervention
options.rationale: The target lesion on ultrasound is already diagnostic. Delay introduces ischaemia risk and is not RCH practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

options.text: Barium contrast enema for combined diagnosis and hydrostatic reduction
options.rationale: Barium has been replaced by air or water-soluble contrast because of peritoneal contamination risk if perforation occurs. The diagnosis is already established; the question is reduction modality. (Source: RCH CPG: Intussusception)

explanation.summary: Ileocolic intussusception classically presents in 3 to 36 month olds with paroxysmal inconsolable crying with leg drawing, pallor, and later redcurrant-jelly stool. Ultrasound shows a target or doughnut lesion and is diagnostic. Treatment of stable cases is pneumatic (air) enema reduction by an experienced paediatric radiologist with surgical backup. Indications for surgery are failed enema reduction, peritonism, perforation, or a pathological lead point.
key_points: Ultrasound target lesion is diagnostic in 3 to 36 month olds with intermittent distress
Pneumatic (air) enema is preferred over hydrostatic; barium is obsolete
Surgery for failed enema, peritonism, perforation, or pathological lead point
IV access and fluid status should be optimised before enema reduction
explanation.pearls: Stable ileocolic intussusception: pneumatic enema reduction under fluoroscopy by a paediatric radiologist. Surgery only for failed enema, peritonism, perforation, or pathological lead point.

stem:
A 9-year-old girl with known asthma is brought to a tertiary emergency department in respiratory distress two hours after she was last seen well at home. She has needed daily salbutamol for a week. She is unable to complete a sentence and has visible tracheal tug and subcostal recession.

She has received continuous nebulised salbutamol with ipratropium for the past hour, oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg 45 minutes ago, and supplemental oxygen. Her SpO2 has only risen from 88% to 91% on Hudson mask and her work of breathing is unchanged on reassessment. The paediatric registrar and intensivist are at the bedside.

data_table.Vital signs: pulse 162 /min, blood pressure 108/72 mmHg, respiratory rate 44 /min, SpO2 91% on Hudson mask 8 L/min, temperature 37.1 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: tracheal tug, subcostal and intercostal recession, widespread expiratory wheeze with quiet areas at both bases, speaking in two-word phrases
data_table.Investigations: venous gas pH 7.30, pCO2 52 mmHg (35 to 45), bicarbonate 22 mmol/L (22 to 32); chest film hyperinflation, no pneumothorax

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next escalation?

options.text: Switch from oral prednisolone to IV methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg six hourly
options.rationale: Oral and IV corticosteroids have equivalent bioavailability for absorbed prednisolone. Switching routes adds no benefit unless the child cannot tolerate oral. The bottleneck here is bronchodilation, not steroid delivery. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: IV aminophylline loading dose 10 mg/kg over 60 minutes followed by an infusion
options.rationale: Aminophylline is reserved by RCH for asthma that has not responded to IV magnesium and continued bronchodilators; it has a narrow therapeutic window and requires levels. Skipping magnesium is not standard practice. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: IV adrenaline 10 micrograms/kg as a slow bolus before further bronchodilators
options.rationale: IV adrenaline is for anaphylaxis-driven bronchospasm or peri-arrest asthma. In severe but not arresting asthma, magnesium and continued nebulisers are the escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with ketamine and rocuronium and elective ventilation
options.rationale: Intubation in severe asthma carries high risk of dynamic hyperinflation and cardiac arrest. It is reserved for failure of medical therapy, exhaustion, or arrest. The child still has gas exchange and effort; magnesium first. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: RCH recommends IV magnesium sulfate as the next escalation in severe acute paediatric asthma not responding to continuous bronchodilators and oral steroids. The dose is 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac and BP monitoring. Aminophylline or IV salbutamol are considered only if magnesium fails. (Source: RCH CPG: Asthma acute)

explanation.summary: Severe paediatric asthma not responding to continuous nebulised bronchodilators and systemic steroids is escalated with IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes. The agent is cheap, widely available, and improves bronchodilation through smooth muscle relaxation. Cardiac and BP monitoring are required because of the risk of bradycardia and hypotension. IV aminophylline or IV salbutamol are reserved for magnesium failure. Intubation carries high peri-intubation arrest risk in asthma and is a last resort.
key_points: IV magnesium 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes is the next step after failed nebulised therapy
Cardiac and BP monitoring during the infusion to detect bradycardia and hypotension
Aminophylline and IV salbutamol come after magnesium failure
Intubation in asthma is high-risk and reserved for exhaustion or arrest
explanation.pearls: Severe paediatric asthma escalation: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg (maximum 2 g) over 20 minutes with cardiac monitoring. Aminophylline only after magnesium failure; intubation is last resort given peri-intubation arrest risk.
